event: The Holy Trinity with the Virgin and Child Enthroned with Saints
symbols: Trinity composition (Father holding the Cross with crucified Son, Dove between) — the Throne of Grace iconographic type, depicting the inner life of the Godhead and the economy of salvation
crucified Christ held by the Father — the Father's offering of His Son for the redemption of the world, the eternal will behind the temporal Crucifixion
dove between Father and Son — the Holy Spirit as the bond of love proceeding from the Father and the Son (filioque)
Virgin Mary with Christ Child below the Trinity — Mary as Theotokos, the human gateway through whom the Second Person enters creation
Archangel Michael with scales — the weigher of souls at the Last Judgment, defender of God's people
Archangel Gabriel with lily — the messenger of the Incarnation, bearing the symbol of Mary's purity
inscription 'AVE MARIA GRA[TIA]' — the angelic salutation of Luke 1:28, linking the scene to the Annunciation
bishop saints with open books — the authority of apostolic teaching and the Fathers who defined Trinitarian doctrine
cherubim surrounding the Trinity — the heavenly court of worship, echoing the seraphim of Isaiah 6
arched format — an altarpiece designed for liturgical veneration, framing the vision as a window into heavenly reality
description: An arched altarpiece composition in two registers. In the upper zone, God the Father, depicted as an elderly bearded figure in golden robes, supports a large crucifix bearing the crucified Christ. A white dove hovers between them. Four winged cherub heads surround the Trinity in a golden aureole. In the lower zone, the Virgin Mary sits enthroned wearing a red dress and deep blue mantle, cradling the nude Christ Child on her lap.
theological_description: This altarpiece unites two of the most important mysteries of Christian faith: the Holy Trinity and the Incarnation through Mary. The upper register employs the 'Throne of Grace' (Gnadenstuhl) iconographic type, rooted in Hebrews 9:14 ('through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God') and formalized in Western art from the 12th century. The Father holds the Cross, the Son hangs upon it, and the Dove-Spirit proceeds between them -- a visual creed expressing the Trinitarian theology defined at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381). This is not merely a depiction of Calvary but of the eternal divine counsel: the Lamb slain before the foundation of the world (Rev 13:8). Below, the Theotokos enthroned with the Child shows the fruit of the Trinity's decision -- the Word made flesh (John 1:14). Archangel Michael with his scales evokes Daniel 12:1 and Revelation 12:7, the heavenly warrior who defends God's people. Gabriel bears the lily and the Annunciation greeting (Luke 1:28), linking the lower register causally to the upper: it was through Gabriel's message and Mary's fiat that the eternal Son took flesh to be crucified. The bishop saints represent the Magisterium that guards and transmits the Trinitarian and Christological faith. Signorelli places the entire economy of salvation -- from the eternal counsel of the Trinity, through the Incarnation, to the eschatological judgment -- within a single unified vision for liturgical contemplation.

scripture_refs.reason: The Trinitarian baptismal formula -- Father, Son, and Holy Spirit -- the foundational text for the doctrine visually expressed in the upper register.
God so loved the world that He gave His only Son -- the Father holding the Cross expresses this divine self-giving.
Through the eternal Spirit Christ offered himself without blemish to God -- the theological basis of the Gnadenstuhl composition showing all three Persons united in the sacrifice.
The Word became flesh -- the lower register of Mary and the Christ Child depicts the Incarnation that the Trinity willed.
Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you -- inscribed on the scroll held by Gabriel, directly linking this scene to the Annunciation.
The Lamb slain from the foundation of the world -- the Crucifixion held within the Trinity expresses not a temporal event but an eternal decree.
Michael and his angels fighting the dragon -- Archangel Michael with his scales represents the heavenly warrior and eschatological judge.
Michael, the great prince who protects God's people -- the archangel in armour fulfills this role of cosmic defender.
The seraphim surrounding God's throne crying 'Holy, holy, holy' -- echoed in the cherubim flanking the Trinity in the golden aureole.
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit -- the Pauline Trinitarian benediction embodied in the composition.

_meta.title: The Trinity, the Virgin and Two Saints
